knowledge.what_it_is: Data analytics is the process of examining data sets to draw conclusions about the information they contain, often using statistical methods and tools such as Excel, SQL, or Python. As a data analyst, you will use data analytics to help organizations make informed business decisions by identifying trends, patterns, and correlations in their data.
what_you_will_learn: Data visualization — creating interactive and dynamic visualizations of data using tools like Tableau, Power BI, or D3.js
Descriptive statistics — calculating measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) and variability (range, variance, standard deviation)
Inferential statistics — using statistical tests (t-tests, ANOVA, regression analysis) to draw conclusions about a population based on sample data
Data cleaning and preprocessing — handling missing values, outliers, and data normalization using techniques like imputation or feature scaling
Data modeling — creating predictive models using linear regression, decision trees, or neural networks to forecast future trends or behavior
SQL querying — writing efficient SQL queries to extract relevant data from databases using joins, subqueries, and aggregations
Data wrangling — transforming and manipulating data into a suitable format for analysis using tools like Pandas or NumPy

knowledge.what_it_is: Data analysis involves extracting insights and meaning from data, typically through statistical methods and visualization techniques. As a data analyst, you will work with various types of data to identify trends, patterns, and correlations.
what_you_will_learn: Data Types — understanding the differences between nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio scales
Data Quality Metrics — calculating measures such as completeness, accuracy, and consistency
Data Visualization Principles — applying best practices for effective data storytelling through plots, charts, and tables
Statistical Measures of Central Tendency — calculating mean, median, mode, and standard deviation
Correlation Coefficient — understanding the concept of correlation and its types (positive, negative, zero)
Data Normalization Techniques — applying methods such as min-max scaling, standardization, and log transformation
Data Imputation Methods — using techniques like mean, median, or regression-based imputation for missing values

knowledge.what_it_is: Data collection involves gathering and storing data from various sources, including databases, APIs, files, and user input. As a Data Analyst, you will be responsible for designing and implementing data collection strategies to support business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: APIs — using RESTful APIs to fetch data from external sources (e.g., Google Analytics API, Facebook API)
Database connections — establishing connections to relational databases (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) or NoSQL databases (e.g., MongoDB, Cassandra)
Data scraping — extracting data from websites using libraries like Beautiful Soup (Python), Scrapy (Python), or Octoparse
File handling — reading and writing data from CSV, Excel, JSON, or other file formats using pandas (Python), NumPy (Python), or R
Web scraping — using tools like Selenium (Python) or Playwright (JavaScript) to automate browser interactions and extract data
Data ingestion pipelines — designing and implementing pipelines for collecting and processing large datasets using Apache NiFi, AWS Glue, or Google Cloud Dataflow
API design principles — following best practices for designing RESTful APIs, including resource naming, error handling, and security considerations
Data quality checks — implementing data validation rules to ensure data accuracy and consistency

knowledge.what_it_is: Data cleanup refers to the process of identifying, correcting, and transforming raw data into a clean and consistent format suitable for analysis. This involves removing errors, inconsistencies, and irrelevant information from datasets.
what_you_will_learn: Handling missing values — strategies for dealing with null or empty cells, including imputation methods (mean, median, mode) and removal techniques
Data normalization — scaling numeric data to a common range using techniques like min-max scaling and standardization
Data encoding — converting categorical variables into numerical representations using one-hot encoding, label encoding, and hash encoding
Duplicate record handling — identifying and removing duplicate rows, including methods for merging and deduplicating datasets
Data validation — implementing checks to ensure data conforms to expected formats and ranges, such as date formatting and numeric range checking
Data profiling — summarizing dataset characteristics using metrics like mean, median, mode, and standard deviation
Data quality metrics — calculating measures of data quality, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score

knowledge.what_it_is: Exploration, also known as data discovery or data exploration, is the process of analyzing and understanding the structure, content, and quality of a dataset to identify patterns, relationships, and trends. As a Data Analyst, you will use various techniques and tools to explore datasets and gain insights that inform business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Data profiling — summarizing key statistics about a dataset, such as count, mean, median, mode, and standard deviation
Data visualization — creating interactive and dynamic visualizations using libraries like Tableau, Power BI, or D3.js to communicate insights effectively
Exploratory data analysis (EDA) techniques — applying statistical methods, such as correlation analysis, regression analysis, and hypothesis testing, to identify relationships between variables
Dimensionality reduction — using techniques like PCA, t-SNE, or MDS to reduce high-dimensional datasets to lower dimensions for easier interpretation
Clustering algorithms — grouping similar data points together based on their characteristics, such as k-means, hierarchical clustering, or DBSCAN
Anomaly detection — identifying unusual patterns or outliers in a dataset using techniques like Isolation Forest or Local Outlier Factor (LOF)
Data sampling and resampling — selecting representative subsets of a dataset to reduce computational costs or improve model performance
Feature engineering — creating new features from existing ones, such as lag variables, moving averages, or interaction terms

knowledge.what_it_is: Data visualisation is the process of creating graphical representations of data to communicate insights and trends. As a Data Analyst, you will use visualisation tools to present findings to stakeholders and inform business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Tableau — creating interactive dashboards with drag-and-drop interface
Power BI — building reports with real-time data updates and DAX formulas
Matplotlib/Pandas Plotting — customising visual appearance and adding annotations
Seaborn/Plotly — creating informative and attractive statistical graphics
D3.js — developing custom interactive visualisations using JavaScript
ggplot2 — creating publication-quality plots with R
Altair — building interactive, web-based visualisations with Python
Bokeh — creating complex, multi-paned dashboards with Python

knowledge.what_it_is: Statistical analysis is a crucial aspect of data analysis, involving the use of statistical methods and techniques to extract insights from data, understand patterns, and make informed decisions. In the context of data analysis, statistical analysis helps to identify trends, relationships, and correlations between variables.
what_you_will_learn: Hypothesis testing — formulating null and alternative hypotheses, calculating p-values, and interpreting results
Confidence intervals — constructing confidence intervals for means, proportions, and regression coefficients
Regression analysis — understanding types of regression (simple linear, multiple linear, logistic), model selection, and interpretation of coefficients
Anova — performing one-way and two-way Anova to compare means across groups, including post-hoc tests
Non-parametric tests — using Wilcoxon rank-sum test, Kruskal-Wallis test, and Spearman's rho for non-normal data
Time series analysis — decomposing time series into trend, seasonality, and residuals, forecasting with ARIMA/ETS models
Correlation analysis — calculating Pearson's r, Spearman's rho, and Kendall's tau to measure linear relationships
Data normalization — applying techniques like min-max scaling, standardization, and logarithmic transformation

knowledge.what_it_is: Machine learning is an application of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve their performance on a task without being explicitly programmed. In the context of data analysis, machine learning algorithms are used to identify patterns, relationships, and trends in large datasets.
what_you_will_learn: Supervised learning — training models on labeled data to make predictions or classify instances
Unsupervised learning — discovering hidden patterns or structures in unlabeled data
Regression analysis — predicting continuous outcomes using linear or non-linear models (e.g. Linear Regression, Decision Trees)
Classification — categorizing instances into predefined classes (e.g. Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines)
Clustering — grouping similar instances together based on their features (e.g. K-Means, Hierarchical Clustering)
Decision trees and random forests — ensemble methods for classification and regression tasks
Gradient boosting machines — an advanced ensemble method for handling complex relationships between variables
Neural networks — a type of machine learning model inspired by the human brain, used for both regression and classification tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: Descriptive analytics provides insights into what happened in the past, analyzing historical data to identify trends, patterns, and correlations. It's a crucial skill for Data Analysts to understand customer behavior, sales performance, and operational efficiency.
what_you_will_learn: Measures of central tendency — mean, median, mode: calculating and interpreting summary statistics
Measures of variability — range, variance, standard deviation: understanding data dispersion and spread
Data visualization techniques — bar charts, histograms, scatter plots: communicating insights effectively
Summary statistics — count, sum, average, minimum, maximum: extracting key metrics from datasets
Data profiling — identifying data types, missing values, and outliers: ensuring data quality and integrity
Correlation analysis — Pearson's r, Spearman's rho: detecting relationships between variables
Regression analysis — simple linear regression, multiple linear regression: modeling relationships between variables
subtopics: summary statistics formulas (e.g. mean, median, mode)
measures of variability calculations (e.g. range, variance, standard deviation)
data profiling techniques (e.g. data cleaning, data normalization)
correlation analysis methods (e.g. pearson's r, spearman's rho)
regression analysis models (e.g. simple linear regression, multiple linear regression)
data quality metrics (e.g. accuracy, precision, recall)

knowledge.what_it_is: Diagnostic analytics involves identifying and resolving issues within an organization's data, processes, or systems. It requires a combination of technical skills, business acumen, and communication abilities to analyze problems, identify root causes, and implement solutions.
what_you_will_learn: Data profiling — understanding data distribution, outliers, and correlations using libraries like Pandas, NumPy, and Matplotlib
Anomaly detection — identifying unusual patterns in data using techniques like One-Class SVM, Local Outlier Factor (LOF), or Isolation Forest
Root cause analysis — applying tools like Fishbone diagrams, 5 Whys, or Pareto charts to identify underlying causes of issues
Data quality metrics — calculating and interpreting metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and mean absolute error using libraries like Scikit-learn
Heatmap visualization — creating interactive visualizations to represent complex data relationships using tools like Tableau, Power BI, or D3.js
Regression analysis — applying linear regression, logistic regression, or polynomial regression to model relationships between variables using libraries like Statsmodels or scikit-learn
Decision trees and random forests — building predictive models to identify patterns in data using algorithms like CART, C4.5, or Random Forest
Clustering analysis — grouping similar data points together based on their characteristics using techniques like K-Means, Hierarchical clustering, or DBSCAN

knowledge.what_it_is: Predictive analytics is a subset of advanced analytics that uses statistical models and machine learning algorithms to forecast future events or behaviors based on historical data. As a Data Analyst, you will use predictive analytics to identify trends, make informed decisions, and optimize business outcomes.
what_you_will_learn: Linear Regression — modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables using ordinary least squares
Decision Trees — building tree-based models that recursively partition data based on feature importance and split criteria
Random Forests — ensemble learning method that combines multiple decision trees to improve predictive accuracy and reduce overfitting
Support Vector Machines (SVM) — finding the optimal hyperplane in a high-dimensional space to separate classes or clusters
Gradient Boosting — iterative model that combines weak predictions from multiple base models to produce a strong prediction
Neural Networks — multilayer perceptron architecture using backpropagation and activation functions to learn complex patterns in data
ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average) — time series forecasting method that models the relationship between current and past values of a time series
Prophet — open-source software for forecasting time series data using generalized additive modeling and seasonality decomposition

knowledge.what_it_is: Prescriptive analytics is a type of data analysis that uses machine learning and optimization techniques to recommend specific actions or decisions, often with the goal of maximizing business outcomes. It involves analyzing past data, identifying patterns and trends, and using this information to predict future outcomes and prescribe optimal courses of action.
what_you_will_learn: Decision trees — a type of supervised machine learning algorithm for building predictive models that can be used for prescriptive analytics
Linear programming — an optimization technique for solving complex problems with multiple variables and constraints, often used in prescriptive analytics to optimize business outcomes
Goal-oriented modeling — a framework for building prescriptive analytics models that take into account specific business goals and objectives
Optimization algorithms — techniques such as linear programming, quadratic programming, and dynamic programming for optimizing business processes and outcomes
Simulation-based optimization — using simulation models to evaluate different scenarios and optimize business outcomes
Prescriptive analytics tools — software platforms such as SAP Analytics Cloud, IBM Planning Analytics, and Oracle Analytics that provide prescriptive analytics capabilities
subtopics: sql queries for data preparation and analysis

knowledge.what_it_is: The IF function in Excel is used to perform logical tests and return one value if true, another value if false. It is a fundamental building block of conditional logic in data analysis.
what_you_will_learn: Logical operators — using AND, OR, NOT to combine conditions
Comparison operators — using =, <>, >, <, >=, <= for exact and relative comparisons
Wildcards — using * and ? for pattern matching in string comparisons
IF-THEN-ELSE statements — nesting multiple IF functions with ELSE clauses
Nested IFs — combining multiple conditions with AND and OR operators
Logical functions — using IF, IFS, and SWITCH to simplify complex conditional logic
Error handling — returning custom error messages or values when conditions are met

knowledge.what_it_is: DATEDIF is a Microsoft Excel function used to calculate the difference between two dates, returning the result in days, months, or years. It is commonly used in data analysis and reporting to measure time-based intervals.
what_you_will_learn: DATEDIF syntax — understanding how to use DATEDIF with A1 notation, specifying date1 and date2 arguments
DATEDIF types — learning the three return types: D (days), MD (months and days), Y (years)
Calculating time intervals — using DATEDIF to find differences between dates in Excel, including absolute and relative time calculations
Handling date formats — understanding how DATEDIF handles different date formats, including serial numbers, text strings, and regional settings
DATEDIF with formulas — combining DATEDIF with other Excel functions, such as IF and SUM, for more complex calculations
Error handling in DATEDIF — learning how to avoid errors when using DATEDIF with invalid or missing dates

knowledge.what_it_is: VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP are lookup functions in Excel used to retrieve data from a table based on a specific value. VLOOKUP looks up a value in the first column of a range, while HLOOKUP looks up a value in the top row of a range.
what_you_will_learn: VLOOKUP syntax — understanding how to use the VLOOKUP function with arguments: lookup_value, table_array, col_index_num, [range_lookup]
HLOOKUP syntax — understanding how to use the HLOOKUP function with arguments: lookup_value, table_array, row_index_num, [range_lookup]
Exact vs. approximate match — choosing between exact or approximate matching in VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP
VLOOKUP vs. INDEX-MATCH — comparing the two methods for performing lookups in Excel
Using VLOOKUP with arrays — understanding how to use VLOOKUP with arrays of values, including multiple criteria
Error handling in VLOOKUP — learning how to handle errors and missing values when using VLOOKUP
Performance optimization — tips for improving the performance of VLOOKUP and HLOOKUP functions

knowledge.what_it_is: The REPLACE and SUBSTITUTE functions in Excel are used to replace or substitute text within a cell or range of cells, with options to specify case sensitivity and wildcards.
what_you_will_learn: REPLACE — replacing old text with new text, preserving formatting and position
SUBSTITUTE — substituting one text for another, also preserving formatting and position
wildcard characters — using asterisks (*) and question marks (?) to match patterns in text
case sensitivity — specifying whether the search is case-sensitive or not
whole words only — searching for whole words instead of partial matches
replacement options — controlling how replacement occurs, such as preserving formatting
array formulas — using arrays to replace or substitute text across multiple cells at once

knowledge.what_it_is: The UPPER, LOWER, and PROPER functions in Excel are used to manipulate text strings by converting them to upper case, lower case, or proper case. These functions are essential for data analysts who need to perform text manipulation tasks.
what_you_will_learn: UPPER function — converts a text string to upper case, replacing all characters with their uppercase equivalents
LOWER function — converts a text string to lower case, replacing all characters with their lowercase equivalents
PROPER function — converts a text string to proper case, capitalizing the first letter of each word and making the rest of the letters lowercase
Text manipulation in Excel — understanding how to use these functions to clean and format text data for analysis
Data formatting best practices — learning when to use UPPER, LOWER, or PROPER to maintain consistency in your reports and dashboards

knowledge.what_it_is: The CONCAT function in Excel is used to combine two or more text strings into one string, often used for data manipulation and reporting.
what_you_will_learn: Using CONCAT with ampersand (&) for simple concatenation — combining two text strings without any formatting
Applying CONCAT with spaces using the & character — adding a space between concatenated text strings
Concatenating multiple columns using CONCAT — combining values from different cells or ranges into one string
Handling missing values in CONCAT using IFERROR or IFBLANK — preventing errors when concatenating empty cells
Using TEXTJOIN instead of CONCAT for more flexible concatenation options — including delimiters and ignoring blank cells

knowledge.what_it_is: The TRIM function in Excel returns a text string that is the result of trimming characters from the start and end of a given text string, leaving only spaces between words.
what_you_will_learn: TRIM — removes leading and trailing spaces from a text string
Trimming vs. cleaning: understanding when to use TRIM versus other functions like CLEAN or SUBSTITUTE for data preparation
Text manipulation with TRIM: applying TRIM to specific columns, rows, or ranges in Excel
Handling mixed data types: using TRIM on numbers, dates, and times to remove unwanted characters
Trimming whitespace from formulas: understanding how TRIM affects formula results and syntax
Using TRIM with other functions: combining TRIM with AVERAGE, COUNT, or SUM for data analysis

knowledge.what_it_is: Charting refers to the process of creating visual representations of data, such as bar charts, line graphs, and scatter plots, to facilitate understanding and analysis. In Excel, charting is a crucial skill for Data Analysts to effectively communicate insights to stakeholders.
what_you_will_learn: Creating column charts — using Excel's built-in Chart Wizard to create simple column charts from data ranges
Customizing chart layouts — adjusting chart titles, axis labels, and legend positions to enhance visual clarity
Using scatter plots for correlation analysis — plotting two variables to identify relationships and patterns in data
Creating stacked charts for categorical data — combining multiple series of data into a single chart to show proportional contributions
Applying conditional formatting to charts — using Excel's built-in formatting options to highlight trends, outliers, or anomalies
Using Power BI to create interactive dashboards — embedding Excel charts within a dynamic and user-friendly interface
Creating heatmaps for matrix data — visualizing large datasets with complex relationships between variables

knowledge.what_it_is: The AVERAGE function in Excel calculates the average (mean) of a set of numbers, often used to summarize large datasets and identify trends.
what_you_will_learn: AVERAGE — calculating the mean of a range or array of numbers
AVERAGEIF — applying conditions to calculate an average based on specific criteria
AVERAGEIFS — extending AVERAGEIF with multiple conditions for more complex calculations
AVERAGEA — handling text and numeric values, returning the average of numeric values only
AVERAGEA with array formulas — using array formulas to calculate averages across multiple ranges or arrays

knowledge.what_it_is: A pivot table is an Excel feature that summarizes and analyzes large datasets by rotating rows into columns or vice versa, allowing users to easily analyze data from multiple angles.
what_you_will_learn: Creating pivot tables in Excel — selecting cells for row labels, column labels, values, and filters
Understanding pivot table fields — creating fields for date, category, and value data types
Using the PivotTable Fields task pane to add/remove fields and change field settings
Configuring pivot table layout options — choosing between tabular, outline, and compact layouts
Creating calculated fields in pivot tables using formulas and functions like SUMIF, AVERAGEIF, and IF
Using Slicers to filter pivot table data visually and dynamically update the table
Understanding and managing pivot table cache sizes for optimal performance

knowledge.what_it_is: The SUM function in Excel is used to calculate the total of a range of cells, often used in data analysis and reporting.
what_you_will_learn: SUM syntax — using =SUM(range) to add up values in a specified range
Absolute vs. relative references — understanding how to use $ to lock cell references
Array formulas — using SUM with array formulas to calculate totals for multiple ranges at once
Named ranges and tables — using named ranges and tables to simplify SUM calculations and improve readability
SUMIF, SUMIFS, and SUMPRODUCT — advanced functions for calculating sums based on conditions and criteria
Dynamic SUM ranges — using formulas like =SUM(A1:A100) to automatically adjust the range as data changes

knowledge.what_it_is: MIN and MAX functions in Excel are used to find the smallest or largest value within a range of cells, often used in data analysis and reporting.
what_you_will_learn: MIN function — returns the smallest number among a set of values, ignoring text and logical values
MAX function — returns the largest number among a set of values, ignoring text and logical values
Applying MIN/MAX to entire columns or rows — using array formulas or built-in functions like MINIFS and MAXIFS for more complex scenarios
Handling missing values with IFERROR and IFBLANK — returning a specific value when an error occurs or a cell is blank
Using MIN/MAX with multiple criteria — applying filters and conditions to find the minimum or maximum value based on multiple factors

knowledge.what_it_is: COUNT is a function in Excel that returns the number of cells in a range that contain numbers, text, or logical values.
what_you_will_learn: COUNTA — counts all non-empty cells in a range, including blank cells
COUNTBLANK — counts the number of blank cells in a range
COUNTIF — counts cells based on multiple conditions, using criteria ranges and operators
COUNTIFS — extends COUNTIF to count cells based on multiple conditions across multiple ranges
COUNT — returns the number of cells in a range that contain numbers
COUNT NUMBERS — an alternative way to write COUNT, which is more explicit about its purpose
COUNT UNIQUE — counts unique values in a range using the FREQUENCY function or the UNIQUE function

knowledge.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language widely used in data analysis, machine learning, and scientific computing. It's known for its simplicity, readability, and large community of developers who contribute to its extensive libraries and frameworks.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and Data Types — understanding Python's basic data types (int, float, str, list, dict, set) and how to declare variables
Operators and Control Flow — mastering arithmetic, comparison, logical, and assignment operators, as well as if-else statements, for loops, while loops, and break/continue statements
Functions — defining and using functions with arguments, return types, and lambda expressions
Modules and Packages — importing and using built-in modules (math, statistics, random) and installing third-party packages via pip
Data Structures — working with lists, tuples, dictionaries, sets, and arrays, including indexing, slicing, and manipulating data
File Input/Output — reading and writing text files, CSVs, and Excel spreadsheets using pandas and openpyxl
Pandas DataFrames — creating, manipulating, and analyzing data using the popular pandas library, including filtering, sorting, grouping, and merging datasets
Data Cleaning and Preprocessing — handling missing values, outliers, and data normalization using pandas and NumPy
Visualization with Matplotlib and Seaborn — creating plots, charts, and heatmaps to visualize data insights
Machine Learning with Scikit-learn — implementing supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms (regression, classification, clustering) using scikit-learn's API
Data Analysis with NumPy and SciPy — performing numerical computations, optimization, and signal processing using NumPy and SciPy libraries

knowledge.what_it_is: R is a popular programming language and environment for statistical computing, data visualization, and data analysis. It was created by Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman at the University of Auckland in 1992 and is now maintained by The R Project for Statistical Computing.
what_you_will_learn: Data types — understanding the different types of variables in R (numeric, character, factor, logical) and how to convert between them
Vectors and arrays — working with vectors and arrays, including indexing, subsetting, and manipulating data structures
Lists and data frames — creating and manipulating lists and data frames, including using the dplyr package for data manipulation
Data visualization — using popular packages like ggplot2, plotly, and Shiny to create interactive visualizations
Statistical modeling — fitting linear models, generalized linear models, and non-linear models using lm(), glm(), and nls() functions
Resampling methods — understanding bootstrapping, cross-validation, and permutation tests for model evaluation
Data manipulation with dplyr — using the pipe operator %>%, group_by(), summarise(), and arrange() to manipulate data
Data visualization with ggplot2 — creating plots with geom_point(), geom_bar(), and facet_wrap() functions
Interactive visualizations with Shiny — building interactive dashboards with reactive expressions and output widgets

knowledge.what_it_is: Pandas is an open-source, BSD-licensed data analysis and manipulation library written in Python, providing high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and operations for efficiently handling structured data, including tabular data such as spreadsheets and SQL tables.
what_you_will_learn: Series — a one-dimensional labeled array of values, similar to an Excel column or a Pandas column
DataFrames — two-dimensional table of values with rows and columns, similar to an Excel spreadsheet or a database table
Indexing and selecting data — using label-based indexing, integer-based indexing, and boolean indexing to access specific rows and columns
Sorting and filtering data — using various sorting algorithms and filtering methods to organize and subset data
GroupBy operations — grouping data by one or more columns and applying aggregation functions such as sum, mean, and count
Merging and joining data — combining DataFrames based on common columns using inner, left, right, and outer joins
Handling missing data — detecting and imputing missing values using various strategies such as mean, median, and interpolation
Data cleaning and preprocessing — removing duplicates, handling outliers, and transforming data types
Data manipulation with merge, join, and concatenate operations — combining DataFrames based on common columns or indices
Reshaping and pivoting data — converting between wide and long formats using melt and pivot_table functions

knowledge.what_it_is: Dplyr is an R package for data manipulation and analysis, providing a grammar-based syntax to perform common tasks such as filtering, sorting, grouping, and joining datasets. It is designed to work seamlessly with the Tidyverse ecosystem and is widely used in data science and analytics roles.
what_you_will_learn: Data frames — working with rectangular data structures in R using dplyr's tbl_df class
Filtering — using %>% and various verbs (e.g., filter(), slice()) to subset data based on conditions
Sorting — arranging data in ascending or descending order using arrange() and desc() functions
Grouping — aggregating data using group_by() and summarizing with summarize() or summarise()
Joining — combining datasets from different sources using inner_join(), left_join(), right_join(), and full_join()
Mutating — adding new columns or modifying existing ones using mutate() and transmute() functions
Selecting — choosing specific variables or rows using select() and slice() functions
Nesting — working with hierarchical data structures using nest() and unnest() functions

knowledge.what_it_is: Matplotlib is a widely used Python library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations. It provides an object-oriented interface for embedding plots into applications using general-purpose GUI toolkits like Tkinter, wxPython, Qt, or GTK.
what_you_will_learn: Plotting basics — creating line plots, scatter plots, bar charts, histograms, and more with matplotlib's pyplot module
Customizing plot appearance — controlling colors, fonts, labels, titles, legends, and axis properties using various options and functions
Working with multiple subplots — arranging plots in a grid or matrix layout to display multiple datasets or views of the same data
Creating interactive visualizations — using tools like Plotly, Bokeh, or Altair to add interactivity to matplotlib plots
Saving and loading plots — exporting plots as images (PNG, PDF, SVG), EPS, or other formats, and reading saved plots back into matplotlib
Handling large datasets — using techniques like sampling, downsampling, or using out-of-core plotting libraries like Biggles or Plotly
Customizing plot layouts — using tools like Seaborn or Plotnine to create publication-quality visualizations with custom themes and styles
subtopics: fonts and font properties (e.g. fontsize, fontname)
labels and titles (e.g. xlabel, ylabel, title)
axis properties (e.g. xticks, yticks, axis labels)

knowledge.what_it_is: Ggplot2 is a powerful data visualization library built on top of R, developed by Hadley Wickham and Winston Chang. It provides a consistent and elegant syntax for creating high-quality visualizations, making it an essential tool for data analysts to effectively communicate insights.
what_you_will_learn: Layers — combining multiple geoms (geometric objects) into a single plot using the + operator
Aesthetics — mapping variables to visual properties such as color, size, and shape
Geoms — creating various types of geometric objects like points, lines, bars, and more
Facets — dividing plots into smaller subplots for comparing multiple groups or categories
Themes — customizing the appearance of plots with pre-defined styles and palettes
Scales — mapping data values to visual properties such as color, size, and shape
Coordinate systems — defining custom coordinate systems for complex layouts and projections
Annotation — adding text labels, arrows, and other annotations to plots

knowledge.what_it_is: A database is a collection of organized data that can be accessed, managed, and updated efficiently. In the context of data analysis, databases provide a centralized repository for storing and retrieving large amounts of structured or semi-structured data.
what_you_will_learn: Relational databases — understanding the concept of tables, rows, columns, primary keys, foreign keys, and SQL queries
NoSQL databases — learning about key-value stores (Redis), document-oriented databases (MongoDB), graph databases (Neo4j), and column-family databases (Cassandra)
Data modeling — creating entity-relationship diagrams to design database schema for efficient data storage and retrieval
Database normalization — understanding the process of organizing data into tables with minimal redundancy and dependency
SQL queries — writing efficient SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements using joins, subqueries, and aggregations
Data warehousing — designing and implementing star/snowflake schema for business intelligence and analytics
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes — understanding the process of extracting data from sources, transforming it into a standardized format, and loading it into a target database

knowledge.what_it_is: A CSV (Comma Separated Values) file is a plain text file used to store tabular data, where each row represents a record and each column represents a field. It's widely used for importing and exporting data between applications.
what_you_will_learn: CSV structure — understanding the format of a CSV file, including headers, rows, and columns
Reading CSV files with pandas — using the `read_csv()` function to load CSV data into a DataFrame
Writing CSV files with pandas — using the `to_csv()` method to save a DataFrame to a CSV file
Handling missing values in CSVs — understanding how to handle missing or null values in CSV files, including strategies for imputation and handling
CSV data types — understanding the different data types that can be stored in CSV files, including integers, floats, strings, and dates
Pivoting CSV data with pandas — using the `pivot_table()` function to transform CSV data from long format to wide format
subtopics: long format vs wide format in csvs

knowledge.what_it_is: APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are sets of defined rules that enable different software systems to communicate with each other, exchange data, and perform specific actions. In a Data Analyst role, APIs are used to fetch data from external sources, integrate with third-party services, and create data pipelines.
what_you_will_learn: API Request Methods — Understanding the HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for interacting with APIs
API Endpoints — Identifying and accessing specific API endpoints for data retrieval or manipulation
API Authentication — Implementing authentication mechanisms (OAuth, JWT, Basic Auth) to secure API access
API Rate Limiting — Understanding rate limiting strategies to prevent excessive API requests and abuse
API Error Handling — Designing error handling mechanisms to catch and handle API-related errors
API Documentation — Creating and consuming API documentation using tools like Swagger or OpenAPI
API Integration with Python Libraries (e.g., requests, pandas) — Using popular Python libraries for API interactions
API Security Best Practices — Implementing security measures (SSL/TLS, encryption) to protect API data

knowledge.what_it_is: Web scraping is the process of automatically extracting structured data from websites, using techniques like HTML parsing and CSS selectors. As a Data Analyst, you'll use web scraping to collect data from public sources, enrich your datasets, and gain insights into business operations.
what_you_will_learn: BeautifulSoup — a Python library for parsing HTML and XML documents, used for web scraping
Requests-HTML — a library that allows you to send HTTP requests and parse HTML responses in a more intuitive way than BeautifulSoup
Scrapy — a full-fledged web scraping framework written in Python, ideal for large-scale data extraction tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: Hypothesis testing is a statistical method used to determine whether there is enough evidence to reject a null hypothesis, often used in data analysis and research to infer population parameters from sample data.
what_you_will_learn: Null Hypothesis — the default assumption that there is no significant difference or relationship between variables
Alternative Hypothesis — the hypothesis that there is a significant difference or relationship between variables, often denoted as H1
p-value — the probability of observing the test statistic under the null hypothesis, used to determine significance
Significance Level (α) — the maximum probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, commonly set at 0.05
Type I Error — incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, also known as a false positive
Type II Error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, also known as a false negative
Confidence Interval (CI) — a range of values within which the population parameter is likely to lie, often used for hypothesis testing
T-Test — a statistical test used to compare the means of two groups, assuming equal variances

knowledge.what_it_is: Dispersion measures how spread out or concentrated a set of data points are from each other, providing insights into variability and stability. It's essential for Data Analysts to understand dispersion metrics as they help identify patterns, trends, and relationships in datasets.

Range — the difference between the largest and smallest values in a dataset, providing a simple yet effective measure of dispersion
Interquartile Range (IQR) — the difference between the 75th percentile (Q3) and 25th percentile (Q1), used to detect outliers and skewness
Coefficient of Variation (CV) — a relative measure of dispersion that compares the standard deviation to the mean, useful for comparing variability across datasets with different scales

knowledge.what_it_is: Variance measures the spread of data from its mean value, indicating how much individual data points deviate from the average. It's a crucial concept in statistical analysis and data science, used to understand the dispersion of data in various fields such as finance, engineering, and social sciences.
what_you_will_learn: Population Variance — formula: σ^2 = (1/n) * Σ(x_i - μ)^2, where n is sample size, x_i are individual data points, and μ is population mean
Sample Variance — formula: s^2 = (1/(n-1)) * Σ(x_i - x̄)^2, where n is sample size, x_i are individual data points, and x̄ is sample mean
Covariance Matrix — a square matrix containing variances and covariances between variables in a multivariate dataset
Variance-Covariance Matrix — a matrix that combines variance and covariance information for multiple variables
Standard Deviation — the square root of variance, used to express dispersion on the same scale as the data
Coefficient of Variation (CV) — ratio of standard deviation to mean, indicating relative variability in a dataset

knowledge.what_it_is: Standard deviation is a measure of dispersion that quantifies how spread out individual data points are from the mean value, used to understand variability and uncertainty in datasets.
what_you_will_learn: Population standard deviation — calculating the square root of the variance for an entire population
Sample standard deviation — estimating the population standard deviation using a sample dataset with n-1 degrees of freedom
Standard error of the mean (SEM) — quantifying the variability of the sample mean, used to construct confidence intervals
Z-scores and T-scores — converting raw data values to standardized scores for comparison across datasets
Coefficient of variation (CV) — expressing standard deviation as a percentage of the mean value, useful for comparing variability between datasets with different units or scales
Standardization by scaling — transforming data using standard scores (Z-scores or T-scores) to have zero mean and unit variance
Robust standard deviation estimation — using methods like interquartile range (IQR) or median absolute deviation (MAD) for datasets with outliers or non-normal distributions

skewness and kurtosis measures of non-normality

In data analysis, understanding distributions is crucial for making informed decisions and predictions.
what_you_will_learn: Normal Distribution — the most common continuous distribution, characterized by its bell-shaped curve and mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ) parameters

knowledge.what_it_is: Central tendency measures the middle value of a dataset, providing a single number that best represents the data set's central or typical value.
what_you_will_learn: Mean — the sum of all values divided by the number of observations, calculated using the formula: x̄ = (Σx) / N

Mode — the most frequently occurring value in a dataset, which may not be unique

what_you_will_learn: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — a measure of average magnitude of errors between predicted and actual values
Mean Squared Error (MSE) — a measure of average squared difference between predicted and actual values, penalizing large errors more heavily than small ones
Root Mean Squared Percentage Error (RMSPE) — a measure of average percentage error between predicted and actual values, useful for evaluating models with varying scales
Coefficient of Determination (R-squared) — a measure of how well the model explains the variance in the target variable, ranging from 0 to 1
Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) — a measure of average percentage error between predicted and actual values, useful for evaluating models with varying scales
Cross-Validation — a technique for evaluating model performance by training and testing on multiple subsets of data, reducing overfitting and improving generalizability
Walk-Forward Optimization — a technique for hyperparameter tuning using time-series data, where the model is trained on past data and evaluated on future data
Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve — a graphical representation of a model's true positive rate vs. false positive rate at different thresholds, useful for evaluating classification models

knowledge.what_it_is: A Decision Tree is a type of supervised learning algorithm used for classification and regression tasks, where each internal node represents a feature or attribute, and each leaf node represents a class label or predicted value. It's a popular choice for data analysts due to its interpretability and ease of implementation.
what_you_will_learn: Decision Tree Induction — the process of creating a decision tree from a dataset using algorithms like ID3 or C4.5
Entropy and Information Gain — understanding how decision trees use these metrics to select the best feature at each node
Pruning — techniques for reducing overfitting in decision trees, such as pre-pruning or post-pruning
Decision Tree Regression — using decision trees for regression tasks, including handling continuous output variables
Feature Selection — methods for selecting the most relevant features to use in a decision tree, such as mutual information or recursive feature elimination
Handling Missing Values — strategies for dealing with missing data in decision trees, including imputation or ignoring missing values
Decision Tree Ensembles — combining multiple decision trees using techniques like bagging or boosting to improve accuracy and robustness

knowledge.what_it_is: Naive Bayes is a family of supervised learning algorithms that use Bayes' theorem with strong independence assumptions to calculate probabilities and make predictions. It's widely used in text classification, spam filtering, and image recognition tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Bayes' theorem — the mathematical formula for updating probabilities based on new evidence
Gaussian Naive Bayes — using a Gaussian distribution to model continuous features
Multinomial Naive Bayes — handling categorical data with multiple classes
Bernoulli Naive Bayes — applying to binary classification problems
Naive Bayes assumption of independence — understanding the limitations and implications
Feature scaling — normalizing feature values for better performance
Hyperparameter tuning — adjusting parameters like alpha, beta, or gamma for optimal results

knowledge.what_it_is: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) is a supervised learning algorithm used for classification and regression tasks, where the goal is to find the closest neighbors of each data point based on their features. It's widely used in various industries such as finance, healthcare, and marketing.
what_you_will_learn: Distance metrics — understanding Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance, Minkowski distance, and their applications
KNN algorithm implementation — learning how to implement KNN using Python libraries like Scikit-learn or Pandas, including handling missing values and outliers

Hyperparameter tuning — understanding the importance of k (number of nearest neighbors) and its impact on model performance, as well as techniques for hyperparameter optimization such as grid search and cross-validation
KNN regression vs classification — learning how to apply KNN for both regression and classification tasks, including handling categorical variables and multi-class problems
Weighted KNN — understanding the concept of weighted KNN, where each neighbor is assigned a weight based on its proximity to the query point
KNN with missing values — learning how to handle missing values in KNN, including imputation techniques and strategies for dealing with incomplete data

knowledge.what_it_is: K-Means clustering is an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that groups similar data points into clusters based on their features, aiming to identify patterns and relationships within the data.
what_you_will_learn: K-Means algorithm — initializing centroids, updating cluster assignments, and convergence criteria
Euclidean distance metric — calculating distances between data points and centroids for clustering
Mean squared error (MSE) — evaluating K-Means model performance using the average squared difference between predicted and actual cluster assignments
Silhouette coefficient — assessing cluster cohesion and separation using a range of -1 to 1, where higher values indicate well-separated clusters
Scikit-learn implementation — using the `KMeans` class from scikit-learn library for K-Means clustering in Python
K-Means++ initialization — using a more robust method to initialize centroids, reducing the risk of poor initializations and improving convergence speed

knowledge.what_it_is: Logistic regression is a supervised learning algorithm used to predict binary outcomes (0/1, yes/no) based on one or more predictor variables. It models the probability of an event occurring using a logistic function, which maps any real-valued number to a value between 0 and 1.
what_you_will_learn: Logistic regression model — understanding the binary outcome prediction problem, coefficients, odds ratios, and confidence intervals
Binary classification metrics — precision, recall, F1 score, ROC-AUC curve, confusion matrix interpretation
Hypothesis testing in logistic regression — p-value calculation, significance level, type I/II errors, and power analysis
Interpretation of coefficients and odds ratios — understanding the effect size, direction, and statistical significance
Feature selection and engineering for logistic regression — selecting relevant predictors, handling categorical variables, and encoding schemes (one-hot, label-encoding)
Regularization techniques in logistic regression — L1/L2 regularization, elastic net, and cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning
subtopics: glmnet package for elastic net regularization

knowledge.what_it_is: Big Data Concepts refer to the fundamental principles and technologies used to manage, process, and analyze large-scale data sets. As a Data Analyst, understanding Big Data Concepts is crucial for designing effective data pipelines, ensuring data quality, and making informed business decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Data Volume — managing petabyte-scale datasets with distributed storage solutions like HDFS, S3, or Azure Blob Storage
Data Variety — handling structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data formats using tools like Apache Avro, Parquet, or JSON
Data Velocity — processing high-speed data streams with technologies like Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, or Amazon Kinesis
NoSQL Databases — designing schema-less databases for flexible data modeling with MongoDB, Cassandra, or Couchbase
Data Warehousing — building centralized repositories for enterprise-wide data integration and analysis using tools like AWS Redshift, Google BigQuery, or Snowflake
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) Processes — implementing data pipelines to extract, transform, and load data from various sources into a target system
Data Governance — establishing policies and procedures for data security, access control, and compliance with regulations like GDPR or HIPAA

Big Data Processing Engines — understanding the differences between batch processing (e.g., Hadoop MapReduce), stream processing (e.g., Apache Flink), and real-time processing engines (e.g., Apache Storm)
Data Quality Metrics — defining and tracking key performance indicators for data accuracy, completeness, and consistency

knowledge.what_it_is: Data storage solutions are technologies and systems used to store, manage, and retrieve large amounts of data efficiently and securely. As a Data Analyst, you will work with various data storage solutions to ensure data integrity, scalability, and performance.
what_you_will_learn: Relational databases — structured data storage using SQL, ideal for transactional data (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL)
NoSQL databases — flexible schema, high scalability, and handling large amounts of unstructured or semi-structured data (e.g., MongoDB, Cassandra)
Cloud-based data warehouses — scalable, secure, and managed services for storing and analyzing large datasets (e.g., Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery)
Data lakes — centralized repositories for raw, unprocessed data from various sources, enabling data discovery and exploration (e.g., Apache Hadoop, Azure Data Lake Storage)
Column-store databases — optimized for analytics workloads, providing faster query performance and reduced storage requirements (e.g., Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery)
Data caching — temporary storage of frequently accessed data to improve query performance and reduce latency
Data replication — maintaining multiple copies of data across different locations or systems for high availability and disaster recovery
Data compression — reducing storage space requirements by compressing data without affecting query performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Hadoop is an open-source, distributed computing framework used for processing and storing large datasets across a cluster of nodes. It was created by Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella and is now maintained by the Apache Software Foundation.
what_you_will_learn: HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System: stores data in a distributed manner using NameNode and DataNodes
MapReduce — programming model for processing large datasets in parallel, consisting of Map and Reduce phases
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) — resource management layer that manages resources and schedules jobs on the cluster
Data Ingestion with Flume: collecting data from various sources and transferring it to HDFS using Flume agents
Data Processing with Pig: high-level language for processing large datasets, using Pig Latin syntax
Data Storage with Hive: metadata management system that provides a SQL-like interface for querying data in HDFS
Hadoop Ecosystem Tools: understanding the role of tools like Sqoop, Oozie, and ZooKeeper in the Hadoop ecosystem

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Spark is an open-source unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing, created by the Apache Software Foundation. It provides high-level APIs in Java, Python, and Scala to process data in parallel across a cluster of nodes.
what_you_will_learn: Spark Core — understanding the core concepts of Spark, including Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs), DataFrames, and Dataset API
DataFrames and Datasets — working with structured data using the DataFrame and Dataset APIs, including creating, manipulating, and querying data
Spark SQL — using the Spark SQL module to query structured data stored in Hive tables or external data sources
Data Manipulation with Spark — using Spark's DataFrames and Datasets to perform common data manipulation tasks such as filtering, sorting, grouping, and joining data
Data Loading and Saving with Spark — understanding how to load and save data from various sources, including CSV, JSON, Parquet, and Avro files
Spark MLlib — using the Machine Learning library (MLlib) for building predictive models, including regression, classification, clustering, and collaborative filtering
GraphX and GraphFrames — working with graph-structured data using the GraphX and GraphFrames libraries
Spark Streaming — processing real-time data streams using Spark's streaming API

knowledge.what_it_is: A neural network is a machine learning model inspired by the structure and function of the human brain, used to analyze and make predictions on complex data. It consists of multiple layers of interconnected nodes (neurons) that process and transmit information.
what_you_will_learn: Multi-layer perceptron — a basic feedforward neural network with one hidden layer, used for classification and regression tasks
Activation functions — mathematical operations applied to neuron outputs, such as sigmoid, ReLU, tanh, and Leaky ReLU
Backpropagation — an optimization algorithm that updates model weights by propagating errors backwards through the network
Forward propagation — the process of feeding input data through a neural network layer by layer to produce output
Gradient descent — an optimization algorithm that minimizes loss function by iteratively adjusting model parameters
Overfitting and underfitting — common issues in neural networks where models either fit training data too closely or fail to capture underlying patterns
Regularization techniques — methods used to prevent overfitting, such as dropout, L1/L2 regularization, and early stopping
Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) — a type of neural network designed for image classification tasks using convolutional and pooling layers

knowledge.what_it_is: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are a type of deep learning model specifically designed to analyze and classify data with grid-like topology, such as images. They are widely used in computer vision tasks like object detection, image segmentation, and facial recognition.
what_you_will_learn: Convolutional layers — applying filters to detect local patterns in images
Pooling layers — downsampling spatial dimensions to reduce feature maps
Activation functions (ReLU, Sigmoid) — introducing non-linearity in CNNs
Batch normalization — normalizing activations to improve stability and speed up training
Max pooling vs. Average pooling — understanding the difference between these two common pooling techniques
Convolutional neural network architectures (LeNet, VGG, ResNet) — learning about popular architectures used for image classification tasks
Transfer learning with pre-trained CNNs (e.g., VGG16, ResNet50) — leveraging pre-trained models for new tasks
Image preprocessing techniques (data augmentation, normalization) — understanding how to prepare images for training

knowledge.what_it_is: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are a type of neural network architecture designed to handle sequential data, such as time series forecasting, natural language processing, and speech recognition. They are particularly useful for modeling temporal dependencies and capturing long-term relationships in data.
what_you_will_learn: Vanilla RNN — basic implementation using simple recurrent connections
LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) cells — memory cells that can learn to forget or remember information over time
GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) cells — a more efficient alternative to LSTMs, with fewer parameters and faster training times
RNN architectures for text classification — using RNNs for sentiment analysis, topic modeling, and named entity recognition
Sequence-to-Sequence models — encoder-decoder architecture for tasks like machine translation and speech recognition
Attention mechanisms in RNNs — using attention weights to focus on relevant parts of the input sequence
RNN implementation in Python using Keras or PyTorch — including data preprocessing, model building, training, and evaluation

knowledge.what_it_is: TensorFlow is an open-source machine learning library developed by Google, widely used for data analysis and modeling tasks in the Data Analyst role.
what_you_will_learn: Keras API — a high-level interface for building neural networks, providing ease of use and flexibility
Tensors — multi-dimensional arrays used to represent data in machine learning models, with operations like matmul and add
Layers — building blocks of neural networks, including convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers
Models — defining and training machine learning models using TensorFlow's Keras API or the low-level Estimator API
Data loading and preprocessing — using tools like tf.data.Dataset for efficient data loading and preprocessing
Model evaluation metrics — understanding and implementing metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and mean squared error
Hyperparameter tuning — using techniques like grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization to optimize model performance

knowledge.what_it_is: PyTorch is an open-source machine learning library developed by Facebook's AI Research Lab (FAIR), used for building and training neural networks. It provides a dynamic computation graph, automatic differentiation, and modular design.
what_you_will_learn: Tensors — multi-dimensional arrays used to represent data in PyTorch, with support for GPU acceleration
Autograd — automatic differentiation system that computes gradients of loss functions with respect to model parameters
Modules — reusable building blocks for neural networks, including linear layers, convolutional layers, and recurrent layers
Loss Functions — common loss functions used in machine learning, such as mean squared error (MSE), cross-entropy loss, and binary cross-entropy loss
Optimizers — algorithms for updating model parameters during training, including stochastic gradient descent (SGD), Adam, and RMSProp
DataLoaders — efficient data loading and batching system that can handle large datasets and distributed training
PyTorch Datasets — pre-built dataset classes for common machine learning tasks, such as MNIST, CIFAR-10, and IMDB
Model Zoo — a collection of pre-trained models and architectures for various tasks, including image classification, object detection, and language modeling

knowledge.what_it_is: Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a subfield of artificial intelligence that deals with the interaction between computers and humans in natural language, enabling machines to process, understand, and generate human-like text. In the context of data analysis, NLP is used for tasks such as sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, and topic modeling.
what_you_will_learn: Tokenization — splitting text into individual words or tokens for further processing
Stopword removal — removing common words like 'the', 'and', etc. that do not add much value to the analysis
Stemming/Lemmatization — reducing words to their base form (e.g., running → run)
Named Entity Recognition (NER) — identifying and categorizing named entities in text, such as people, organizations, and locations
Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging — identifying the grammatical category of each word in a sentence (e.g., noun, verb, adjective)
Sentiment Analysis — determining the emotional tone or sentiment behind a piece of text (positive, negative, neutral)
Topic Modeling — extracting underlying topics or themes from a large corpus of text
Dependency Parsing — analyzing the grammatical structure of sentences and identifying relationships between words

knowledge.what_it_is: Parallel processing is an essential technique in data analysis, where multiple tasks are executed simultaneously to improve computational efficiency and reduce processing time. It's particularly useful when dealing with large datasets or complex computations.
what_you_will_learn: Multi-threading — creating multiple threads within a single process to execute tasks concurrently
Distributed computing — dividing tasks among multiple machines or nodes in a cluster for parallel execution
MapReduce — a programming model and software framework used for processing large data sets in parallel
Data Parallelism — splitting data into smaller chunks and processing them simultaneously using multiple cores or threads
Task Parallelism — executing multiple independent tasks concurrently to improve overall throughput
OpenMP — a widely-used API for parallelizing loops and regions of code on shared-memory architectures
MPI (Message Passing Interface) — a standard library and communication protocol for distributed memory parallel computing
PySpark — an Apache Spark module for Python that enables parallel processing of large-scale data sets using the Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDD) API

knowledge.what_it_is: Message Passing Interface (MPI) is a standardized and widely-used library for parallel computing, allowing multiple processes to communicate efficiently across distributed memory architectures.
what_you_will_learn: Process management — creating, spawning, and managing MPI processes using MPI_Init(), MPI_Comm_spawn(), and MPI_Finalize()
Point-to-point communication — sending and receiving messages between processes using MPI_Send(), MPI_Recv(), and MPI_Isend()
Collective operations — synchronizing data across multiple processes with functions like MPI_Bcast(), MPI_Gather(), and MPI_Allreduce()
Data types and datatypes — understanding MPI_Datatype, MPI_Type_create_struct(), and MPI_Type_commit() for efficient data transfer
Non-blocking communication — using MPI_Irecv(), MPI_Isend(), and MPI_Wait() to overlap computation with communication
Error handling and debugging — using MPI_Errhandler_set(), MPI_Comm_get_errhandler(), and MPI_Query_thread() to handle errors and debug issues

knowledge.what_it_is: MapReduce is a programming model and software framework used for processing large data sets in parallel across a cluster of computers, commonly used in Hadoop ecosystem. It consists of two main components: Map and Reduce.
what_you_will_learn: Map function — transforming input data into key-value pairs using user-defined functions (UDFs), typically implemented in languages like Java or Python
Reduce function — aggregating the output from map tasks, applying a reduction function to produce the final result, often used for grouping and summing operations
Mapper configuration — specifying input/output formats, partitioning, and other settings for efficient data processing
Reducer configuration — defining shuffle and sort parameters, as well as output format specifications
JobTracker and TaskTracker — understanding the roles of these components in managing MapReduce jobs and tracking task progress
Input/Output Formats — learning about various formats like SequenceFile, TextFile, and Avro for efficient data storage and retrieval
MapReduce programming model — applying the map-reduce paradigm to solve real-world problems, such as data aggregation and filtering

knowledge.what_it_is: Image recognition is a type of machine learning (ML) technique used to identify and classify objects within images, commonly employed in data analysis tasks such as image classification, object detection, and facial recognition.
what_you_will_learn: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) — the primary architecture for image recognition tasks, consisting of convolutional and pooling layers
Image Preprocessing — techniques to normalize and enhance images, including resizing, cropping, and data augmentation
Transfer Learning — leveraging pre-trained models like VGG16, ResNet50, or MobileNet for image recognition tasks
Object Detection using YOLO (You Only Look Once) or SSD (Single Shot Detector) algorithms
Facial Recognition using FaceNet or OpenFace libraries and techniques such as face alignment and feature extraction
Image Segmentation — techniques to identify and label specific objects within images, including semantic segmentation and instance segmentation
Deep Learning frameworks like TensorFlow, PyTorch, or Keras for building and training image recognition models
